steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The hypothesis is that Ag-NP bioaccumulation in P. pruinosus can serve as a proxy for X. laevis — meaning measurements in the invertebrate must reliably predict measurements in the frog. A weakening finding must break that predictive link.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'P. pruinosus accumulates more types of nanoparticles than X. laevis': Describes a breadth difference but says nothing about whether Ag-NP levels in one species predict levels in the other. ✗
  • Choice B — 'P. pruinosus accumulates Ag-NPs with fewer negative effects than X. laevis': Addresses tolerance, not whether concentrations correlate. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Ag-NP concentrations in P. pruinosus are not predictive of Ag-NP concentrations in X. laevis': Directly severs the predictive link the proxy depends on — if the invertebrate's readings don't forecast the frog's readings, the proxy is worthless. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Concentrations in both species show little individual variation': Low within-species variance would actually support the proxy hypothesis by making readings more reliable. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
